fuzzy_prd:
We need a small utility that helps our configuration-driven framework decide where to look for optional plug-in components. Right now the framework doesn't have a clean way to express which component-discovery strategy should be used, and different host environments have different capabilities — some expose the running process's loaded libraries while others do not. We want a closed, enumerated set of named discovery strategies so that application authors can declare their preferred approach in a config file without writing any custom code. There should be exactly two strategies: the default one that trusts whatever is already loaded in the running process (this is what we should use unless told otherwise), and an alternative that always walks the working directory for library files on disk — handle the fallback the same way as the assembly-loader module does today. Each strategy must have a stable name, a stable numeric position, and we must be able to identify which one is the default. We also need a small command-line resolver: you pass in a strategy name (or its numeric position), and it prints back the canonical name, the numeric position, and whether it is the default. The output format must be exact key=value lines, one per attribute, with no extra whitespace or metadata.

A couple small clarifications from the questions that came back: the resolver output needs to be exactly three lines, in this order: 'name=<canonical identifier>\nvalue=<ordinal as decimal integer>\ndefault=<true or false>\n'. Each line is plain key=value with no spaces around the equals sign, and there does need to be a trailing newline after the last line too.

Also, the only two canonical identifiers are exactly 'UseLoadedAssemblies' and 'AlwaysScanDllFiles', and those names need to stay stable and match exactly in output. 'UseLoadedAssemblies' is ordinal 0 and default=true. 'AlwaysScanDllFiles' is ordinal 1 and default=false. And yes, choosing the mode by its ordinal string should behave the same as choosing it by name, so '"0"' should resolve exactly like 'UseLoadedAssemblies' and '"1"' should resolve exactly like 'AlwaysScanDllFiles'.

trigger_keywords: what is the input format for the resolver command
how does the CLI take its input
what JSON fields does the program read
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The program reads a single JSON object from stdin with exactly two fields: 'op' (always the string 'describe') and 'selector' (the mode identifier string or ordinal string).

trigger_keywords: what happens when no mode is specified
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: UseLoadedAssemblies is the default mode. It occupies ordinal 0. Its 'default' field in output is 'true'. AlwaysScanDllFiles is ordinal 1 and default=false.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle the fallback the same way as the assembly-loader module does today
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The AlwaysScanDllFiles mode mirrors the fallback behavior in DllScanningAssemblyFinder.cs, which scans .dll files in the working directory when GetEntryAssembly returns null

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_default_loaded_assemblies_mode..description: Resolving the default assembly-discovery mode of the two-mode selector. This mode instructs the configuration reader to discover candidate assemblies from those already present in the running process rather than scanning files on disk. It is the first-declared member of the selector, so it owns ordinal 0 and is the value chosen when no mode is supplied. The resolver echoes the canonical mode identifier, its ordinal, and whether it is the default. The selector may be given as the mode identifier or as its ordinal.

feature2_explicit_dll_scanning_mode.json.description: Resolving the explicit on-disk scanning mode of the two-mode selector. This mode instructs the configuration reader to always discover candidate assemblies by scanning library files in the working directory instead of relying on assemblies already loaded in the process. It is the second-declared member of the selector, so it owns ordinal 1 and is not the default. The resolver echoes the canonical mode identifier, its ordinal, and whether it is the default. The selector may be given as the mode identifier or as its ordinal.
